data.title: Equities are ‘priced for perfection’ and vulnerable to a correction – Goldman
data.description:
Equities are priced for perfection with the S&P 500’s (SP500) rally in prices and having risen 23% last year after a 24% return in 2024, said Goldman Sachs.

The rise in stock prices during the past two years has been in the 93rd percentile over same periods in the last 100 years, said Peter Oppenheimer, chief global equity strategist at Goldman Sachs’s Global Investment Research, in a note.

“While we expect equity markets to make further progress over the year as a whole – largely driven by earnings – they are increasingly vulnerable to a correction driven either by further rises in bond yields and/or disappointments on growth in economic data or earnings.”

In addition, he said that three factors are complicating the setup for equities in 2025:

  1. How fast stock prices have risen recently, affecting much of the goods news expected on growth.
  2. High valuations are likely limiting forward returns.
  3. Unusually high market concentration is increasing portfolio risks, including increased U.S. dominance over the rest of the world, concentration within sectors – such as technology vastly dominating market returns – and the Magnificent Seven stocks, accounting for nearly a quarter of the index.

The top seven companies within the S&P 500 (SP500) rose by 47%, compared with 10% for the median company within the index.

About 46% of the S&P 500 (SP500) return was generated by only five companies – Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Broadcom (AVGO) – which added $6T in market value, “equivalent to the total size of the UK (EWU), (FLGB) and German (EWG), (DAX) stock markets.”

Lastly, Oppenheimer said that “a simple comparison of the most cyclical versus the most defensive parts of the market illustrates that much of the strength in equities in recent months has reflected higher growth expectations, particularly in the U.S. where optimism on U.S. deregulation and tax cuts have also played a role.”

“This leaves equities vulnerable to any growth disappointments, particularly depending on specific policy measures by the incoming U.S. administration in relation to tax and tariff decisions.”

data.title: Conservative network Newsmax to raise more money due to high investor demand ahead of IPO
data.description:
Newsmax has upsized its preferred offering to $175M after the conservative U.S. network raised more money than it previously anticipated ahead of its initial public offering.

CEO Chris Ruddy said in a statement this week that Newsmax raised $153M, exceeding target by $3M over the Christmas-New Year’s holiday from high investor interest, and hence decided to raise more money.

Mark Elenowitz, managing director of Digital Offering Bank, which is handling the private and public raise for the network, said more than 85,000 investors have expressed interest in participating in the current private placement or the IPO.

“The amount of interest is nothing that we've ever seen. We are urging everyone who wants to invest and are eligible to do so now during the extension period,” Elenowitz said.

Newsmax said it expects to go public, subject to SEC approval, in the next couple of months on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol “NMAX.”

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: December nonfarm payroll growth is expected to moderate further
data.description:
The U.S. labor market has eased since its overheated state in 2021 and 2022. And the Department of Labor's release of its jobs report on Friday is expected to show further softening, but not a collapse.

Economists expect the U.S. economy will add 157K jobs to nonfarm payrolls in December, slowing from the initial November estimate of 227K. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate is expected to remain at 4.2%.

Scott Wren, senior global market strategist at Wells Fargo Investment Institute, will be looking at the wage data in the report. He thinks the consensus estimate for a 4% Y/Y increase will be key. "If you see an average hourly earnings number that comes in on a year-over-year basis comes in below 4%, you'll probably see stocks rally."

And conversely, "if it comes in above 4%, you might see the S&P 500 trade a little bit lower because I think that wage component is important, and it's something that the market's watching."

"Reports showing a healthy hiring pace, lower unemployment, and strong wage growth could lead the market to push the 10-year Treasury rate toward 5% in the short term," wrote Michael Kramer of Mott Capital Management, Investing Group Leader for Reading The Markets. That could reduce expectations for rate cuts, suggesting that the Federal Open Market erred in 100 basis points of rate cuts since September, Kramer added.

The Federal Open Market Committee's Summary of Economic Projections showed that, on average, Fed members expect two rate cuts this year, down from four in its September SEP. Wells Fargo only has one rate cut penciled in for 2025, Wren said.

For the payrolls number, Wren is expecting that160K-170K jobs were added in December, roughly in line with consensus.

Wren expects further deterioration in the labor market, with a year-end 2025 U.S. unemployment rate of 4.8%. Still, under 5% unemployment still represents a healthy economy, compared with the historical average of 5.7%.

The U.S. economy and labor market have held up better than many economists, and Wren, specifically, had expected, as consumer spending has exceeded expectations, supported by the upper end of the wage spectrum.

While Wren expects U.S. GDP growth to moderate from its 2024 pace, likely about 2.7%, "it doesn't appear as though it's going to really fall out of bed," he said.

Chris Lau, Investing Group Leader for DIY Value Investing, expects the December data to show continued job growth, especially in the government, healthcare, and leisure sectors. Thus, he suggests that investors consider boosting positions in such areas as defense and healthcare, while being cautious about retail and energy.

SA Analyst Damir Tokic sees signs of a recession already appearing, noting an increase in the number of unemployed people over the past year, a decrease in employed people, and unemployed people having a harder time finding a new job. And while there's still positive job growth, those new jobs are largely skewed towards healthcare and government jobs, which are more related to demographics and government spending than economic strength, he wrote earlier this week.

Wren sees a resurgence of inflation as the biggest threat to the economy. Currently, Wells Fargo is expecting the consumer price index to rise ~3% in 2025, higher than the 2.5% consensus. So inflation rising "noticeably higher" than his expectations would be a worry. Stronger-than-expected global economic growth could fuel inflation, especially in the second half of the year, he noted. "I think that would be in my mind that's the biggest risk out there," he said.

For a more near-term view of inflation, stay tuned for next week's release of the December consumer price index reading.

data.title: Southwest Airlines CFO to retire
data.description:
• Southwest Airlines (NYSE:LUV) said on Thursday that Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Tammy Romo and Chief Administration Officer Linda Rutherford will retire from their positions effective April 1, 2025.
  • Romo joined Southwest Airlines in 1991 and has held many leadership roles in the past 33 years, including Head of Investor Relations, Controller, Treasurer, and Senior Vice President Planning.She became Executive Vice President & CFO in 2012.
  • Southwest will begin a search for Romo's replacement.

data.title: Intel could announce new CEO in 'a couple of months,' Citi says
data.description:
• Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) could be on the verge of announcing a new, permanent CEO in "a couple of months," Citi said on Thursday.
  • Analyst Christopher Danely, who has a Neutral rating on the semiconductor giant, met with several members of the company at the Consumer Electronics Show and got the inclination a new leader could be announced in short order. Danely also said that Intel is working on streamlining its operations with a goal of gaining market share in the CPU market in 2025, which he said is "the right thing to do."
  • Former Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger abruptly retired last month (though some believe he was forced out) and has been replaced on an interim basis by co-CEOs David Zinsner and Michelle Johnston Holthaus.

data.title: Riley Exploration Permian declares $0.38 dividend
data.description:
• Riley Exploration Permian (NYSE:REPX) declares $0.38/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 4.62%
  • Payable Feb. 6; for shareholders of record Jan. 23; ex-div Jan. 23.
  • See REPX Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Ubisoft hires advisors for review of strategic options
data.description:
French video game maker Ubisoft (OTCPK:UBSFY) said it has hired advisers to review its strategic options.

"Ubisoft announced today that it has appointed leading advisors to review and pursue various transformational strategic and capitalistic options to extract the best value for stakeholders," the video game maker said in a statement on Thursday.

The announcement comes after media reports last year that the founding family and Tencent Holdings (OTCPK:TCEHY) were working on a potential buyout. Reuters reported last month that Ubisoft's founding Guillemot family has been in talks with Tencent about funding a management-led buyout of the video game maker. Tencent is Ubisoft's second-largest holder.

The company also announced Thursday that it's delaying the release of the latest edition of its Assassin's Creed video game by a month. The release of "Assassin's Creed Shadows" is now scheduled for March 20.

Q3 FY 2024-25 net bookings are now expected at ~300 million primarily reflecting lower than expected holiday sales, mainly for Star Wars Outlaws, as well as the discontinuation of XDefiant. Ubisoft still expects approximately break-even non-IFRS operating income and free cash flow forFY2024-25. Net bookings are now expected to come in at around €1.9 billion.

Ubisoft (OTCPK:UBSFY) also said it now expects to exceed €200 million in reduction of fixed cost based by FY2025-26 vs. FY2022-23 on an annualized basis.

data.title: Amazon Buy with Prime expands to Fossil brand
data.description:
• Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) Prime shoppers can now buy watches and accessories directly from Fossil.com through Amazon’s “Buy with Prime” platform.
  • Beginning in February, Prime members can enjoy the same benefits such as free and same-day delivery, easy returns, and 24/7 support through the Fossil.com website.
  • Fossil will offer all its styles and options available through Buy with Prime, including best-selling watches, handbags, and jewelry, enabling shoppers to add directly to their Amazon shopping carts and fulfilled through Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN) fulfillment centers.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Mortgage rates continue to inch up, supply remains an issue
data.description:
Mortgage rates continued to climb up, as strength of the economy put upward pressure, while lack of entry-level supply remained an issue, the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey showed.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.93% as of January 9, up from 6.91% last week and 6.66% in the year-ago period.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.14%, compared to 6.13% a week ago and 5.87% a year ago.

"In the first full week of the new year, the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage remained elevated at just under 7 percent,” Sam Khater, Freddie Mac’s Chief Economist, said.

“The continued strength of the economy has put upward pressure on mortgage rates, and along with high home prices, continues to impact housing affordability. The lack of entry-level supply also remains an issue, especially for those looking to become first-time homeowners,” Khater added.

data.title: Globus Medical upgraded to neutral by BoA on Q4 performance
data.description:
BofA Securities has upgraded Globus Medical (NYSE:GMED) to neutral from underperform, citing the company's strong Q4 revenue performance.

The investment bank noted that Globus's (NYSE:GMED) preannounced Q4 revenue was 3% above Street expectations. The company also introduced 2025 revenue and EPS forecasts that were both approximately 1% above Street midpoints.

BofA said it now sees "less integration risk" following the Q4 preannouncement and that "any potential disruption for 2025 is contemplated in the guidance."

"The guide implies topline acceleration which we see as a sign of management's confidence it can deliver on high cross-selling expectations," BofA added.

The bank raised its price target for the stock to $97 from $80.
